one_liner: A 68-year-old woman presents with 6 weeks of bilateral shoulder and hip girdle pain and stiffness, worst in the morning. She has trouble combing her hair and rising from a chair.

stage2.hpi_extension: She describes aching and stiffness in both shoulders and hips that is worst on waking and lasts for over an hour before gradually improving. She has difficulty lifting her arms above her head to comb her hair and getting out of low chairs without using her arms. She denies frank muscle weakness. She also notes low-grade fevers, fatigue, and a 2-kg weight loss. Importantly, she has no headache, jaw claudication, scalp tenderness, or visual changes. No distal joint swelling.
stage2.surgical_history: Right total knee arthroplasty 5 years ago
stage2.social_history: Retired teacher. Lives independently. Non-smoker, rare alcohol.
stage2.physical_exam: General: well-appearing elderly woman in no acute distress. Musculoskeletal: markedly limited active range of motion of both shoulders and hips due to pain, with preserved passive range. Muscle strength is normal (5/5) when formally tested once pain is controlled. No joint swelling, synovitis, or deformity. No scalp or temporal artery tenderness. Temporal arteries are non-tender with normal pulses. Normal neurologic exam.

lab_results.clinical_significance: Markedly elevated ESR and CRP in an older patient with proximal girdle pain and stiffness are the hallmark laboratory finding of polymyalgia rheumatica.

lab_results.result: Creatine kinase (CK) normal. Liver enzymes, creatinine, electrolytes within normal limits.
lab_results.clinical_significance: A normal CK helps distinguish polymyalgia rheumatica from inflammatory myopathies such as polymyositis and dermatomyositis, which typically present with proximal muscle weakness and elevated CK.

lab_results.result: Rheumatoid factor negative. Anti-CCP negative. ANA negative.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Negative autoantibody testing argues against seropositive rheumatoid arthritis and other connective tissue disease. Polymyalgia rheumatica is a clinical diagnosis supported by elevated inflammatory markers and response to low-dose steroids.

stage5.final_clue: She is started on low-dose prednisone (15 mg daily) and experiences dramatic improvement of her pain and stiffness within 48 hours, with normalization of her ESR over the next 2 weeks.

debrief.classic_presentation: Polymyalgia rheumatica occurs almost exclusively in patients over 50, with a female predominance. Patients present with bilateral shoulder and pelvic girdle pain and stiffness, particularly in the morning, often lasting more than an hour. Patients describe subjective weakness, but on examination strength is preserved once pain is overcome. Constitutional symptoms are common. Roughly 15% of patients with PMR develop giant cell arteritis, and physicians must screen for headache, jaw claudication, and visual symptoms at every visit.
debrief.pathophysiology: PMR is thought to represent a systemic inflammatory syndrome involving the synovium and periarticular structures (bursae, tendons) of the proximal girdles rather than a primary muscle disease. The pathogenesis overlaps with giant cell arteritis and is driven by IL-6 and other pro-inflammatory cytokines. There is no specific autoantibody, and muscle enzymes are characteristically normal.
debrief.treatment_overview: Low-dose oral prednisone (10-20 mg daily) produces dramatic clinical improvement within 24-72 hours, which is considered diagnostic. Steroids are then tapered slowly over 1-2 years based on symptoms and ESR/CRP. Methotrexate or tocilizumab can be used as steroid-sparing agents. Patients should be monitored closely for features of giant cell arteritis during follow-up, and for steroid-related complications (osteoporosis, hyperglycemia).
high_yield_pearls: PMR = pain and stiffness without true weakness; polymyositis = weakness with elevated CK.
Dramatic response to low-dose prednisone within 1-3 days supports the diagnosis and is essentially diagnostic.
Up to 15% of PMR patients develop giant cell arteritis; always screen for headache, jaw claudication, and visual changes.
CK is normal in PMR; an elevated CK should prompt evaluation for inflammatory myopathy.
ESR is classically markedly elevated (often >50 mm/hr) but can occasionally be normal in early disease.
sources: First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Musculoskeletal, Skin, and Connective Tissue chapter, 'Polymyalgia rheumatica' section.
